1: Isaiah 27:1 applies the ANE cosmic combat mythology to YHWH's eschatological victory: 'bayom hahu yifqod YHWH becherbo haqqasheh vehagedolah vehachazaqah, in that day YHWH will visit with his sword, the hard and the great and the strong one.' The three-fold description of the sword (hard = qasheh; great = gadol; strong = chazaq) emphasizes its irresistibility. 'Al-livyatan nachash bariach ve'al-livyatan nachash aqalaton veharag et-hatannin asher bayam, against Leviathan the fleeing serpent and against Leviathan the coiling serpent; and he will slay the dragon in the sea.' Leviathan (livyatan) appears in the Ugaritic Baal Cycle as Lotan (lt-n) — Baal's great sea-enemy with seven heads — whom Baal defeats. The two Leviathans ('fleeing/runaway' and 'twisted/coiling') are the two epithets of the Ugaritic Lotan. YHWH here claims the cosmic dragon-combat for himself. Revelation 12:9 identifies the dragon as the ancient serpent/Satan.
2: 'In that day, sing about it: A vineyard of choice wine!' — the second vineyard song (cf. Isaiah 5:1-7, the first). The contrast is complete: the first vineyard produced bad fruit and was destroyed (Isaiah 5:2-7); this vineyard is 'of choice wine' (chemd, desire/delight). 'Bayom hahu kerem chemd anu lah, in that day, a vineyard of choice wine — sing about it!' The singing command is joyful: the damaged vineyard of Isaiah 5 is now the delightful vineyard of Isaiah 27. The vine (Israel) that had been devastated by judgment is now restored and productive. The joy of the new vineyard song is the response to Leviathan's defeat (Isaiah 27:1) — when the chaos-monster is slain, the vineyard flourishes.
3: 'I, the LORD, tend it; I water it constantly; so that nothing can hurt it, I guard it day and night' — YHWH as the perfect vinedresser: 'ani YHWH notzerah liraga'im ashqennah, I the LORD keep it; moment by moment I water it.' The 'moment by moment' watering (liraga'im, at every moment) emphasizes constant divine care. 'Pen yifqod aleihah lailah veyomam entsurennah, lest anything visit it — night and day I guard it.' The all-day-all-night guardianship contrasts with the first vineyard's guardian who went away (Isaiah 5:2: 'then he went away'). The theme of YHWH as the shepherd-guardian who does not sleep (Psalms 121:3-4: 'He who watches over Israel will neither slumber nor sleep') applied to the vineyard-watching.
4: 'I have no wrath against my vineyard. If briers and thorns came against me in battle, I would stride through them and burn them all down' — the absence of wrath: 'chemah ein li, I have no wrath' (chemah = the hot anger of divine jealousy, the burning passion). The briers and thorns (shamir vashayit — the judgment-vegetation of Isaiah 5:6; Isaiah 7:23-25; Isaiah 9:18; Isaiah 10:17) now come not from YHWH against the vineyard but from enemies against the vineyard's keeper. YHWH's response: 'eflasah bah achadah ashitenah yachdav, I would stride through it as a unit, I would burn them together.' The vineyard's enemies are burned rather than the vineyard itself being burned (as in Isaiah 5:6: 'I will make it a wasteland... no rain').
5: 'Or else let him come to me for refuge; let him make peace with me — yes, let him make peace with me' — the alternative to burning: the open invitation to peace. 'O yachazek bema'uzi yaaseh shalom li, or let him grasp my refuge/stronghold, let him make peace with me.' The double 'let him make peace with me' (shalom yaseh-li shalom yaseh-li) is an emphatic invitation: even the briers and thorns (enemies) can choose peace over burning. This is the paradox of divine wrath alongside divine invitation: the same God who burns his enemies invites them to come for refuge. The 'grasping the stronghold' = the faith-act of running to YHWH for protection rather than fighting against him.
6: 'In coming days Jacob will take root; Israel will blossom and sprout and fill the whole world with fruit' — the eschatological fruitfulness of the restored vineyard: 'habba'im yashresh Ya'aqov, in coming days Jacob will take root.' The metaphor develops: ya'aqov yashresh = Jacob takes root (the deep underground work of establishing the plant); yatsis ufarak Yisrael = Israel blossoms and flowers; umile'u venei tevel tenuvah = and they fill the face of the world with fruit. The three stages (root, blossom, fruit-filling) describe the progressive eschatological fruitfulness. 'Fill the world with fruit' = the Abrahamic multiplication blessing (Genesis 1:28; Isaiah 12:3; Isaiah 22:17-18) realized through Israel as the fruit-bearing vine. John 15:5 applies this vineyard fruitfulness to the disciples united to Christ.
7: 'Has God struck Israel as he struck those who attacked Israel? Has Israel been killed as she killed her enemies?' — the comparative-proportionality question: 'hekimakkath-makkehv hikahv, is his striking like the striking of his striker?' The question probes the proportionality of Israel's judgment versus the judgment of her enemies. The implied answer: No — Israel's judgment was measured/proportionate (Isaiah 27:8: 'by measure he dealt with her') while the enemies were completely destroyed. The differentiated judgment reflects the covenant relationship: Israel, as YHWH's own, receives corrective discipline (musar = parental correction); the enemies receive destruction. Amos 3:2: 'You only have I chosen of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities.'
8: 'By measured exile he dealt with Israel; he removed her with his fierce wind — the scorching east wind' — the 'measured exile' of Israel: 'besa'assah beshale'ah terivennah, by measure, by driving out, you contend/quarrel with her.' The disciplinary exile is measured — not total annihilation (as with Assyria or Babylon) but a carefully proportioned removal. 'Hagas beruchot kashshah beyom qadim, he expelled her with his fierce wind, on the day of the east wind.' The east wind (qadim) is the sirocco that blows off the desert — hot, dry, destructive (Jonah 4:8; Job 27:21; Jeremiah 18:17). The exile as a divine wind-blast: forceful but controlled.
9: 'By this, then, Jacob's guilt will be atoned for; this is the full result of the removal of his sin: he will crush the altar stones to chalk, and no Asherah pole or incense altar will stand' — the exile as the instrument of atonement: 'lakhen beze yechupar avon Ya'aqov, therefore by this Jacob's guilt will be atoned' (yechupar from kaper = to cover/atone). The exile accomplishes what centuries of voluntary repentance had not: the removal of idolatry. 'He will crush all the altar stones like chalk' (vesam kol-avnei mizbeach ke'avnei-gir menupatsot, he will make all the altar stones like chalk stones crushed). The Asherah poles (asherim) and incense altars (chammanim) removed — the objects that constituted Israelite apostasy (2 Kings 23:4-15: Josiah's reform destroyed exactly these objects, partially fulfilling the oracle).
10: 'The fortified city stands deserted, its dwellings abandoned like a wilderness — there calves graze and lie down, stripping its branches bare' — the desolate city as a sign of completed judgment: 'ki ir betsura badad mivshar meshulach veneerav, for the fortified city is deserted, a lonely dwelling, abandoned like a wilderness.' The domestic animals inhabiting the ruins: 'sham yireh egel vesham yirbets vekilah seifoteiyha, there calves will graze and there lie down and consume its branches.' The calves consuming the city's vegetation = the complete takeover of human space by agricultural use (the city reverting to farmland). This image appears throughout the judgment oracles (Isaiah 13:20-22; Isaiah 17:2; Isaiah 34:13-15).
11: 'When its branches wither, they are broken off; women come and make fires with them. For this is a people without understanding, so their Maker will not have compassion on them, and their Creator will show them no mercy' — the final judgment on the people 'without understanding': 'bifshel qetzira teissaber nashim bao me'irot otah, when its branch is dry it is broken; women come, making fire with it.' The dried branch used as firewood = the utterly useless remnant of the apostate city. The diagnosis: 'ki lo am beinot hu, for this is a people without understanding.' The lack of understanding (beinot = discernment/insight) is the fundamental prophetic diagnosis of Israel's failure (Isaiah 1:3: 'Israel does not understand'). 'Al-ken lo yerachemannu osehv, therefore his Maker will not have compassion on him; veyotsero lo yechunnenu, and his Creator will show him no mercy.'
12: 'In that day the LORD will thresh out his grain from the Euphrates to the Brook of Egypt, and you will be gathered up one by one, O people of Israel' — the eschatological ingathering as threshing: 'vehayah bayom hahu yachbot YHWH mishipbolet hannahar, in that day YHWH will thresh from the flowing of the River' (hannahar = the Euphrates, the great river of Mesopotamia). 'Ad-nachal Mitsrayim, to the brook of Egypt' (the Wadi el-Arish, the traditional southwestern boundary of the land). The entire territory from the northeast boundary (Euphrates) to the southwest boundary (Brook of Egypt) = the full Promised Land extent (Genesis 15:18). 'And you will be gathered one by one, O people of Israel' (velaqutem lachat echad benei Yisrael, and you will be gathered, one by one, O children of Israel) — the careful, individual gathering contrasts with the mass deportations of exile.
13: 'In that day a great trumpet will sound, and those who were perishing in the land of Assyria and those exiled in Egypt will come and worship the LORD on the holy mountain at Jerusalem' — the great trumpet of ingathering: 'vehayah bayom hahu yittaqa beshofar gadol, in that day a great shofar will be blown.' The shofar gadol (great ram's horn) is the signal of the eschatological gathering (Matthew 24:31: 'he will send his angels with a great trumpet call'; 1 Thessalonians 4:16: 'with the trumpet call of God'; 1 Corinthians 15:52: 'the last trumpet will sound'). Those 'perishing in Assyria' and 'exiled in Egypt' = the northern exiles (722 BCE deportation to Assyria) and those who fled to Egypt. 'They will worship the LORD on the holy mountain at Jerusalem' — the eschatological worship on Zion fulfills the pilgrimage theology of Isaiah 2:2-4.
